touchpoints.positive_design_opportunity: Clear explanation of diagnostic uncertainty and next steps
touchpoints.data_needed: Common patient misunderstandings during first neuro consult

touchpoints.positive_design_opportunity: Provide medication expectation framing and monitoring plan
touchpoints.data_needed: Drop-off rates after first-line therapy initiation

touchpoints.moment: Symptoms fail to improve or worsen
touchpoints.data_needed: Average time to reassessment after non-response
Top search queries during diagnostic uncertainty

touchpoints.positive_design_opportunity: Caregiver-facing question checklist for second opinions

touchpoints.moment: Referral delay to movement disorder specialist
touchpoints.data_needed: Average referral lag time in target geographies

sponsor_actions.action: Develop educational materials acknowledging diagnostic complexity
Train investigators to explicitly address prior misdiagnosis trauma
Frame trial as second-chance clarity rather than experimental risk

profile.name: Karl W.
profile.quote: Something feels off, but I keep telling myself it’s probably nothing serious.
profile.bio_1: Karl began experiencing unexplained neurological symptoms that disrupted his daily life about ten years agfo. After a years-long diagnostic odyssey involving multiple specialists, he was finally diagnosed with a rare condition.
profile.bio_2: Karl prefers to approach healthcare choices analytically and often researches medical literature and engages heavily in online patient groups before making decisions about his treatment options.
profile.goal1: Understand the full range of treatment options and how they might affect long-term outcomes.
profile.goal2: Maintain his professional productivity and independence despite symptoms.
profile.goal3: Identify clinical trials that could provide earlier access to promising therapies.
profile.barrier1: Conflicting information from different physicians about diagnosis and disease progression.
profile.barrier2: Difficulty balancing medical appointments with a demanding job schedule.
profile.barrier3: Uncertainty about how symptoms may progress and impact his future career.

journey.narrative_description: Karl first notices subtle balance changes and fatigue. He is concerned but assumes it may be age-related. He trusts her providers and believes the issue will be manageable.
journey.quote: Something feels off, but I keep telling myself it’s probably nothing serious.
sentiment_reasons: Symptoms are mild and attributed to normal aging
Karl retains full trust in his care team at this stage
Daily function remains largely intact

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Underlying worry is present but well-managed beneath a surface of optimism.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Karl enters the healthcare system with strong baseline faith in his physicians.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: He can articulate his concerns but lacks a framework for what the symptoms might mean.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Work schedule and geographic access to care are not yet a limiting factor.

journey.narrative_description: When symptoms fluctuate or temporarily improve, Karl feels reassured. Hope increases and He interprets the instability as something benign or reversible.
journey.quote: When it gets a little better, I convince myself I might be overreacting.
sentiment_reasons: Temporary symptom relief creates false reassurance
Fluctuation interpreted as proof the problem is benign
Emotional burden briefly lifts during improvement windows

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Relief from fluctuating symptoms generates genuine, if premature, optimism.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Improvement is attributed to providers' guidance, temporarily reinforcing trust.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Karl feels less urgency to seek information during calm periods, slightly reducing engagement.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Fewer symptoms mean fewer appointments needed, reducing scheduling pressure.

journey.narrative_description: During early appointments, Karl struggles to clearly describe her symptoms. He leaves feeling partially heard but uncertain. Trust begins to soften as explanations feel incomplete.
journey.quote: I’m trying to explain what’s happening, but I’m not sure I’m being understood.
sentiment_reasons: Appointments feel inconclusive and leave questions unanswered
Karl struggles to articulate neurological symptoms accurately
Provider communication feels rushed or incomplete

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Frustration with incomplete answers begins to erode the early optimism.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Karl leaves appointments feeling partially heard but not fully understood.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Difficulty describing symptoms makes him doubt his own ability to communicate effectively.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Appointments are manageable but starting to compete with work obligations.

journey.narrative_description: As symptoms continue progressing, Karl feels discouraged and confused. The lack of clarity lowers her confidence in her own understanding and in the care pathway.

inflection_detail.label: Care Pathway Fork
inflection_detail.description: Worsening symptoms with no clear explanation force a decision: continue with the current provider and wait, or escalate toward specialist care. The choice made here directly determines how long Karl remains undiagnosed.
inflection_detail.sponsor_opportunity: Provide primary care physicians with a structured symptom-progression checklist that triggers automatic neurology referral when motor symptoms persist beyond 90 days.
inflection_detail.data_needed: Median time between first reported motor symptom and specialist referral in community care settings.

paths.label: Escalates to Specialist
paths.outcome: Referral to a movement disorder neurologist is initiated. Diagnostic timeline shortens by an estimated 6–12 months, preserving provider trust and patient confidence.
paths.key_driver: Family pressure and persistent symptom logging prompt action.

paths.label: Waits and Watches
paths.outcome: Continued regression without specialist input deepens despair and erodes medical self-efficacy. Misdiagnosis risk increases significantly.
paths.key_driver: Provider reassurance and logistical friction delay escalation.

journey.quote: Things are getting worse and I still don’t have answers—it’s starting to wear on me.
sentiment_reasons: Continuous worsening without explanation generates despair
Absence of a clear diagnosis creates a sense of system failure
Karl begins questioning his own perception of symptoms

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Progressive decline without answers shifts mood from frustration toward fear and hopelessness.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Repeated inconclusive visits erode confidence in the current care pathway.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Karl loses confidence in his ability to self-advocate or meaningfully influence the trajectory.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Increasing symptom burden begins interfering with work and daily scheduling.

journey.narrative_description: The referral to a neurologist restores cautious optimism. Karl believes expert input may finally provide answers, even though the logistics feel burdensome.
journey.quote: Seeing a specialist finally feels like a step in the right direction.
sentiment_reasons: Specialist referral feels like a meaningful step forward
Karl interprets the referral as validation that his concerns are serious
Anticipation of expert input restores cautious hope

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The prospect of expert evaluation provides emotional relief after a period of stagnation.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Primary care physician's willingness to refer partially restores confidence in the system.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Karl feels neither more nor less capable — awaiting the specialist's interpretation.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Specialist appointment adds scheduling complexity and potential travel burden.

journey.narrative_description: Receiving an initial but incorrect diagnosis is destabilizing. Karl feels surprised and unsettled. When treatment does not help, doubt grows—both in the system and in her own interpretation of symptoms.
journey.quote: If this diagnosis is wrong, what else have we missed?
sentiment_reasons: Incorrect diagnosis triggers surprise and disorientation
Treatment non-response deepens doubt in clinical judgment
Karl begins questioning whether the system can be trusted to find the answer

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Receiving a wrong diagnosis and watching treatment fail is deeply destabilizing emotionally.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: A misdiagnosis is a concrete breach — Karl's trust in specialist accuracy drops sharply.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Confusion about what is actually happening undermines his ability to participate in decisions.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Treating a wrong diagnosis wastes time, financial resources, and scheduling bandwidth.

journey.narrative_description: Seeking a second opinion feels proactive. Karl regains some agency and begins researching more deeply, determined to reach diagnostic clarity.
journey.quote: I need to take control and make sure we’re not overlooking anything.
sentiment_reasons: Pursuing a second opinion restores a sense of personal agency
Karl feels he is taking control of a process that felt out of his hands
Research-oriented approach aligns with his analytical identity

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Taking proactive steps lifts mood from passive despair toward purposeful engagement.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust is rebuilt cautiously — directed at the new provider rather than the system broadly.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Active research and self-advocacy significantly increase Karl's sense of capability.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Second opinion requires additional appointments and potential travel, adding logistical strain.

journey.narrative_description: The Parkinson’s diagnosis brings both relief and grief. Relief that there is finally a label; grief over what it implies. He feels overwhelmed by the long arc of disease progression.

inflection_detail.label: Diagnosis Decision Point
inflection_detail.description: Receiving a confirmed Parkinson's diagnosis is the central inflection of Karl's journey. How He and her care team respond in the immediate aftermath — emotionally, informationally, and logistically — determines whether He moves toward empowered self-management or retreats into isolation and fear.
inflection_detail.sponsor_opportunity: Develop a diagnosis-day support package for HCPs: standardized emotional framing language, a patient-facing 'What happens next' one-pager, and a warm handoff to a patient navigator or advocacy organization.
inflection_detail.data_needed: Rate of clinical trial awareness at time of confirmed PD diagnosis; proportion of patients who receive disease management education within 30 days of diagnosis.

paths.label: Supported Transition to Self-Management
paths.outcome: Karl receives structured post-diagnosis education, is connected to an advocacy community, and begins shared decision-making with her neurologist. Trial awareness is introduced within 60 days.
paths.key_driver: Proactive HCP communication, patient navigator support, and strong family involvement.

paths.label: Emotional Withdrawal and Disengagement
paths.outcome: Without structured support, grief dominates. Karl delays follow-up appointments, withdraws from her caregiver network, and is unlikely to engage with trial options for 12+ months.
paths.key_driver: Information overload at point of diagnosis, lack of emotional scaffolding, and unaddressed logistical concerns.

journey.quote: At least I have a name for it now, but I’m not sure what comes next.
sentiment_reasons: Confirmed diagnosis delivers relief after years of uncertainty
Grief over the chronic, progressive nature of Parkinson's follows quickly
Overwhelming information at point of diagnosis creates cognitive overload

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The emotional weight of a Parkinson's diagnosis — relief mixed with grief — produces a net negative valence.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust in the diagnosing neurologist wavers as Karl processes whether care has been timely enough.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: The scope and complexity of PD management feels far beyond his current knowledge base.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Anticipating long-term specialist visits, medication management, and potential symptom interventions overwhelms near-term planning.

journey.narrative_description: Online communities provide emotional validation and practical insights. Karl feels less alone and gains confidence in navigating future decisions.
journey.quote: Hearing from others like me makes this feel a little less isolating.
sentiment_reasons: Peer connection with others living with Parkinson's reduces isolation
Practical strategies from community members increase confidence
Shared experiences normalize the diagnosis and provide emotional validation

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Community belonging and validation produce a meaningful uplift in overall emotional state.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Positive stories about care experiences in the community reinforce trust in specialist-led treatment.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Absorbing peer knowledge about managing PD symptoms substantially grows Karl's self-management confidence.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Community members share practical workarounds for appointment logistics and medication management.

journey.narrative_description: When evaluating clinical trials, Karl feels cautiously hopeful. He trusts her neurologist but worries about the physical and logistical demands participation may require.
journey.quote: I want to try something new, but I need to be sure I can handle what it takes.
sentiment_reasons: Clinical trial option represents hope for disease-modifying benefit
Karl trusts his neurologist's guidance but is cautious about commitment
Logistical demands of trial participation create real uncertainty

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Cautious hope about potential trial benefit is moderated by anxiety about what participation requires.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Karl relies on his neurologist's recommendation as the primary filter for trial evaluation.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Research experience from earlier stages means Karl can meaningfully evaluate trial options.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Trial visit schedules, geographic constraints, and work balance create tangible participation barriers.

profile.name: Candace D.
profile.quote: There were months I don't remember at all. I had to learn who I was again — and now I'm trying to figure out if there's something that can actually stop this from coming back.
profile.bio_1: Candace was a second-year PhD candidate when she experienced what her family described as a psychiatric break — erratic behavior, paranoia, and seizure-like episodes that escalated over several weeks. After hospitalization, she was diagnosed with anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis.
profile.bio_2: She spent nearly four months inpatient, including three weeks in the ICU. Now in partial recovery, Candace manages residual cognitive fatigue, word-finding difficulties, and anxiety that she and her care team attribute to post-encephalitis syndrome. She has resumed light coursework but is not yet back to full academic capacity. She is highly motivated to understand her disease and advocates for herself in clinical settings, though she sometimes struggles with memory and processing speed during complex conversations.
profile.goal1: Achieve full cognitive recovery and return to her doctoral program without permanent academic setback.
profile.goal2: Reduce relapse risk and understand whether maintenance immunotherapy or a clinical trial offers better long-term protection.
profile.goal3: Rebuild a sense of identity and continuity after a period of significant memory loss and personality disruption.
profile.barrier1: Rarity of the disease means few local neurologists have meaningful experience treating anti-NMDAR encephalitis beyond acute stabilization.
profile.barrier2: Cognitive fatigue and residual word-finding difficulties make it hard to fully engage in complex medical conversations or parse dense clinical trial protocols.
profile.barrier3: Deep anxiety about relapse and uncertainty about whether current stability reflects true remission or temporary suppression.

journey.narrative_description: Candace and her family notice mood changes, insomnia, and increasingly disorganized thinking. Initial consultations with a campus mental health counselor result in a provisional anxiety and stress diagnosis. Candace complies but feels that something more serious is being missed.
journey.quote: I knew it wasn't just stress. I've been stressed before. This was different.

events.label: Dismissed as Anxiety
events.description: Campus counselor attributes symptoms to graduate school stress. Candace is given coping strategies but no medical referral, delaying workup by several weeks.
events.tooltip: Stress and anxiety misdiagnosis delayed medical workup by several weeks, allowing symptoms to worsen unchecked.

sentiment_reasons: Symptoms are attributed to stress, creating false normalcy
Candace's own intuition conflicts with the clinical assessment
Family is beginning to notice changes but no formal concern is raised

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Mounting unease about dismissed symptoms produces low-grade but persistent distress.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Initial trust in the campus counselor is intact but Candace privately questions the framing.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Candace is self-aware enough to sense misalignment but lacks the clinical vocabulary to challenge it.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Academic and care logistics are still manageable — this has not yet become a crisis.

journey.narrative_description: Within weeks, Candace experiences auditory hallucinations, catatonic episodes, and a witnessed seizure in her apartment. Her partner calls 911. She is admitted to an inpatient psychiatric unit before transfer to a general neurology service. Candace has limited memory of this period.
journey.quote: I barely remember the first few weeks. I've had to piece it together from my mom's notes.

events.label: 911 Call / Emergency Admission
events.description: Partner calls 911 after witnessed tonic-clonic seizure. Candace is transported by ambulance and admitted to an inpatient psychiatric unit before neurology transfer.
events.tooltip: Witnessed seizure triggers 911 call. Admitted to psychiatric unit before neurology transfer — a common and costly misrouting.

events.label: Routed to Psychiatric Unit First
events.description: Initial psychiatric placement delays neurological workup by 48–72 hours. Symptom escalation during this window increases severity of the acute episode.
events.tooltip: Psychiatric placement delays neurological workup 48–72 hours, worsening the acute episode before a correct unit transfer.

sentiment_reasons: Rapid escalation to psychosis and seizure is traumatic and terrifying
Candace loses cognitive continuity during this period
Family is thrust into an emergency without preparation or understanding

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: An acute psychiatric emergency represents the lowest emotional point — terror, confusion, and loss of self.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Psychiatric misrouting before neurology transfer introduces early distrust in the system's ability to identify her condition.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Candace is cognitively absent during this stage — she cannot participate in her own care.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Emergency admission collapses all normal logistics — academic, social, and personal capacity drops to zero.

journey.narrative_description: Candace is intubated and receives high-dose steroids and IVIG in the ICU. Her mother becomes the primary decision-maker during this period. The medical team explains anti-NMDAR encephalitis to her family, though Candace herself is not cognitively present for these conversations.
journey.quote: My mom said the doctors finally had a name for it. I was there but I wasn't really there.

events.label: ICU Intubation
events.description: Respiratory compromise leads to intubation. Candace is placed on mechanical ventilation and receives high-dose steroids and IVIG. Her mother becomes sole medical decision-maker.
events.tooltip: Respiratory compromise leads to intubation in the ICU. Candace's mother becomes sole medical decision-maker.

events.label: Anti-NMDAR Antibody Confirmed
events.description: CSF analysis confirms anti-NMDA receptor antibodies. The team delivers a definitive diagnosis to Candace's family, replacing weeks of diagnostic uncertainty with a named condition and a treatment roadmap.
events.tooltip: CSF analysis confirms anti-NMDA receptor antibodies — replacing weeks of diagnostic uncertainty with a named condition and treatment roadmap.

sentiment_reasons: ICU admission and intubation represent extreme medical severity
Candace is not cognitively present for the diagnostic disclosure
Her mother becomes the sole decision-maker, adding caregiver stress to the system

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Candace is sedated and absent, but retrospective awareness of this period generates deep grief and anxiety.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The ICU team's competence and the eventual confirmed diagnosis begin to rebuild some institutional trust.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Candace cannot advocate for herself — her agency is entirely delegated to her mother.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: All capacity is consumed by acute medical management; normal life is fully suspended.

journey.step: Tumor Workup and Negative Ovarian Teratoma Screen
journey.narrative_description: Imaging and surgical evaluation reveal no underlying teratoma, which the team presents as a favorable prognostic signal. Candace's family receives this news with cautious relief. Candace begins a slow return to awareness and can hold brief conversations.
journey.quote: No tumor. That was the first thing that felt like good news.

events.label: Teratoma Ruled Out
events.description: Pelvic MRI and surgical oncology consultation confirm no underlying ovarian teratoma. The medical team presents this as a favorable prognostic signal. Candace's family exhales.
events.tooltip: No underlying teratoma found on imaging. Team presents this as a favorable prognostic signal; family receives first genuinely good news.

events.label: Candace Regains Awareness
events.description: Candace begins holding brief, coherent conversations for the first time in weeks. She recognizes her mother and partner. Care team marks this as a meaningful clinical milestone.
events.tooltip: Candace holds brief, coherent conversations for the first time in weeks — a key clinical milestone marking neurological stabilization.

sentiment_reasons: Absence of teratoma is the first unambiguously good news
Candace begins to regain awareness and can recognize family members
Cautious optimism emerges but the long road ahead is still unclear

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The first positive clinical finding — no tumor — generates meaningful emotional relief.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The oncology and neurology team's thoroughness increases confidence in the diagnostic process.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Candace begins to return but cannot yet process complex information or participate in decisions.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Still inpatient — logistical capacity remains severely constrained despite improving medical status.

journey.narrative_description: After incomplete response to first-line therapy, the neurology team initiates rituximab. Candace is now cognitively present enough to be partially involved in consent discussions, though she relies heavily on her mother. She experiences fatigue and nausea but gradually stabilizes.
journey.quote: They explained what rituximab was. I remember nodding and not really absorbing all of it.

events.label: Incomplete Response to First-Line Therapy
events.description: Steroids and IVIG do not produce sufficient clinical improvement. The team escalates to rituximab, extending hospitalization and introducing new side effect concerns for Candace and her family.
events.tooltip: First-line steroids and IVIG produce insufficient improvement, requiring escalation to rituximab and extending hospitalization.

events.label: Stabilization on Rituximab
events.description: After two rituximab infusions, Candace shows measurable neurological improvement. Cognitive responsiveness increases and motor symptoms begin to recede. The care team considers discharge planning.
events.tooltip: Two rituximab infusions yield measurable neurological improvement. Cognitive responsiveness increases and discharge planning begins.

sentiment_reasons: First-line failure introduces new uncertainty and extends hospitalization
Rituximab stabilization eventually provides measurable reassurance
Candace can partially participate in consent but relies heavily on her mother

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Incomplete first-line response triggers anxiety, but subsequent stabilization prevents a deeper emotional slide.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The team's willingness to escalate treatment demonstrates clinical attentiveness, sustaining trust.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Partial cognitive return allows Candace to begin absorbing information, though processing is still impaired.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Extended hospitalization continues to disrupt all external obligations and family resources.

journey.narrative_description: After nearly four months, Candace is discharged with an outpatient immunology follow-up plan and neuropsychological testing scheduled. Her family receives limited structured guidance on what recovery should look like at home. She moves back with her mother.
journey.quote: They said I was stable enough to go home. I didn't feel stable — I just wasn't ICU-level anymore.

events.label: Discharge Without Recovery Roadmap
events.description: Candace is discharged stable but without structured written guidance on what recovery milestones to expect, what symptoms warrant re-admission, or how to access mental health support for post-encephalitis syndrome.
events.tooltip: Discharged without written recovery milestones, re-admission criteria, or mental health referral — leaving Candace and her family without a structured roadmap.

sentiment_reasons: Discharge without a structured recovery roadmap creates anxiety about what comes next
Candace does not feel medically stable, only less acute
Family receives minimal guidance on expected recovery milestones

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The gap between 'stable enough to discharge' and 'feeling recovered' produces lingering emotional uncertainty.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Discharge without clear written guidance slightly reduces trust in care continuity planning.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Candace is cognitively present but unsure how to manage symptoms or recognize warning signs at home.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Transitioning home introduces new logistical demands — follow-up scheduling, medication management, and family coordination.

journey.narrative_description: Neuropsychological testing reveals deficits in processing speed, working memory, and verbal fluency. Candace finds the results both validating and devastating — the data confirms what she has been experiencing, but also documents how far she is from her prior baseline. She begins occupational and speech therapy.
journey.quote: Seeing the numbers made it real. I knew I was slower, but now I had proof.

events.label: Neuropsych Testing Reveals Deficits
events.description: Formal testing documents significant impairments in processing speed, working memory, and verbal fluency relative to pre-illness baseline. Candace finds the results validating but devastating.
events.tooltip: Testing documents significant deficits in processing speed, working memory, and verbal fluency — confirming what Candace felt but making it clinically undeniable.

events.label: Begins Occupational + Speech Therapy
events.description: Candace begins a structured rehabilitation program. Early sessions are cognitively exhausting but begin to show incremental improvement in word retrieval and task sequencing.
events.tooltip: Structured rehab program begins. Early sessions are exhausting but show incremental gains in word retrieval and task sequencing.

sentiment_reasons: Neuropsychological testing confirms deficits that Candace already suspected
Documentation of how far she is from baseline is both validating and devastating
Beginning structured rehabilitation introduces purpose and a recovery framework

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Having deficits clinically confirmed is emotionally mixed — validation plus confrontation with loss.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The rehabilitation team's structured approach to her recovery increases trust in the outpatient care system.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Formal assessment results give Candace a factual baseline she can use to track progress.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Outpatient therapy is manageable but requires ongoing scheduling coordination across multiple providers.

journey.narrative_description: Every new headache, word-retrieval failure, or sleep disturbance triggers fear of relapse. Candace begins tracking symptoms obsessively in a notes app and contacts her neurologist frequently. The care team provides reassurance but limited structured guidance on relapse warning signs.

inflection_detail.label: Hypervigilance Inflection
inflection_detail.description: Candace's post-discharge symptom monitoring behavior reflects both high health literacy and unmanaged relapse anxiety. This is a critical window where structured support could channel her vigilance productively — or, without intervention, escalate to avoidance and psychological burden.
inflection_detail.sponsor_opportunity: Provide a structured relapse early-warning framework — co-developed with patients — that distinguishes normal recovery fluctuation from actionable warning signs. A digital symptom tracker with embedded clinical guidance could reduce anxiety-driven contacts while increasing meaningful signal capture.
inflection_detail.data_needed: Percentage of anti-NMDAR encephalitis patients who experience clinically documented relapse within 24 months; rate of anxiety disorder comorbidity in post-encephalitis populations.

paths.label: Structured Monitoring Engagement
paths.outcome: Candace's vigilance is formalized into a symptom-tracking protocol with her care team. She receives psychoeducation on distinguishing recovery variation from relapse signals, reducing acute anxiety while maintaining appropriate monitoring. She becomes an engaged candidate for prevention-focused clinical trials.
paths.key_driver: Clear relapse criteria provided by HCP team; access to a nurse navigator or disease-specific patient coordinator; peer connection with other anti-NMDAR encephalitis survivors.

paths.label: Anxiety Escalation and Disengagement
paths.outcome: Without structured guidance, Candace's self-monitoring becomes destabilizing. She begins avoiding activities that could trigger perceived symptoms, withdraws from academic reintegration, and grows skeptical of trial participation due to fear of adverse events destabilizing her fragile recovery.
paths.key_driver: Lack of post-discharge relapse education; no mental health co-management; perceived dismissiveness from neurology team regarding ongoing anxiety.

journey.quote: Every time something feels off, I spiral. Is this the beginning of another episode? How would I even know?

events.label: Relapse Fear Triggers Symptom Spiral
events.description: A minor headache and word-retrieval lapse send Candace into hours of obsessive symptom tracking and an urgent call to her neurologist. No relapse is occurring, but the anxiety becomes debilitating.
events.tooltip: A minor headache triggers hours of obsessive symptom tracking and an urgent call to her neurologist — no relapse, but the anxiety becomes destabilizing.

events.label: No Structured Relapse Warning Protocol
events.description: Candace's care team provides verbal reassurance but no written framework distinguishing normal recovery fluctuation from true relapse signals. This gap sustains her anxiety and generates repeated unplanned contacts.
events.tooltip: No written framework provided to distinguish normal recovery fluctuation from true relapse signals, sustaining hypervigilance and unplanned care contacts.

sentiment_reasons: Every normal symptom triggers fear that it is a relapse signal
Obsessive monitoring becomes exhausting and destabilizing
The care team provides verbal reassurance but no structured framework

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Hypervigilance and relapse anxiety produce persistent negative emotional baseline despite physical stability.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Repeated unscheduled contacts with the neurology team sustain trust but reveal gaps in proactive relapse guidance.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Candace is highly engaged in monitoring but lacks a clinical framework that would make her vigilance productive.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Anxiety-driven contacts and self-monitoring are consuming significant mental and logistical bandwidth.

journey.narrative_description: Candace discovers the Autoimmune Encephalitis Alliance and multiple patient-run online communities. Reading others' recovery narratives provides her first real sense of what the trajectory can look like. She begins contributing, sharing her own experience, and asking detailed questions about treatment maintenance and trial options.
journey.quote: I found people who had been where I was and made it back further than I thought was possible. That changed everything.

events.label: Finds Autoimmune Encephalitis Alliance
events.description: Candace discovers the AE Alliance and connects with other anti-NMDAR encephalitis survivors online. Reading full recovery stories for the first time fundamentally shifts her sense of what is possible.
events.tooltip: Connecting with AE Alliance survivors — including people who returned to graduate school — fundamentally shifts Candace's sense of what recovery is possible.

events.label: Peer Mentorship with Fellow Survivor
events.description: An AE Alliance volunteer who recovered from anti-NMDAR encephalitis and returned to graduate school reaches out directly. Their exchange becomes a monthly touchpoint that sustains Candace's motivation during cognitively difficult weeks.
events.tooltip: Monthly peer mentorship with an AE Alliance volunteer who completed a PhD post-encephalitis sustains motivation through cognitively difficult weeks.

sentiment_reasons: Finding others who have recovered from anti-NMDAR encephalitis fundamentally shifts Candace's sense of what is possible
Peer mentorship with a survivor who returned to graduate school provides direct role modeling
Community belonging reduces isolation and sustains motivation

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Community discovery produces the most significant positive emotional shift in Candace's entire journey.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Community members validate the care Candace has received and provide context for the recovery process.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Absorbing peer knowledge about managing residual symptoms substantially boosts Candace's self-management confidence.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Community members share practical strategies for managing appointments, travel, and academic reintegration.

journey.narrative_description: Motivated by community members' experiences and her own desire to access novel maintenance therapies, Candace begins reviewing ClinicalTrials.gov listings. She identifies several trials investigating B-cell depletion maintenance and neonatal Fc receptor (FcRn) antagonists for autoimmune encephalitis but struggles to parse eligibility criteria and endpoints.
journey.quote: I can find the trials. I just can't always tell if I qualify, or what I'd actually be signing up for.

events.label: Identifies Relevant Trial on ClinicalTrials.gov
events.description: Candace finds an active Phase II trial investigating FcRn antagonist maintenance therapy in anti-NMDAR encephalitis. She bookmarks the listing and prepares questions for her next neurology appointment.
events.tooltip: Candace identifies an active Phase II FcRn antagonist maintenance trial on ClinicalTrials.gov and prepares questions for her next neurology appointment.

events.label: Eligibility Criteria Too Complex to Parse
events.description: The inclusion/exclusion criteria reference biomarker thresholds and prior treatment windows Candace cannot evaluate without clinician interpretation. She cannot confirm her own eligibility unaided.
events.tooltip: Inclusion/exclusion criteria reference biomarker thresholds Candace cannot evaluate without clinician help — she cannot confirm eligibility unaided.

sentiment_reasons: Identifying a relevant trial on ClinicalTrials.gov generates genuine excitement
Eligibility criteria are too complex to parse without clinical guidance
Candace feels motivated but blocked by information access barriers

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The prospect of accessing a maintenance therapy trial produces cautious but real optimism.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Candace recognizes she needs her neurologist to interpret eligibility, reinforcing the provider relationship.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: High health literacy enables meaningful trial identification, but biomarker criteria exceed her self-interpretation capacity.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Geographic distance to trial sites introduces a logistical barrier Candace and her mother begin calculating.

journey.narrative_description: Candace raises trial participation with her neurologist, who is supportive but candid about the limited trial infrastructure available locally. She is referred to a specialist at an academic medical center three hours away who has more experience with the disease and active trial affiliations. The added travel burden concerns her but does not deter her.
journey.quote: My doctor was honest — she said she wasn't the right person to guide me on trials. I appreciated that more than I expected.

events.label: Referred to Academic Medical Center
events.description: Local neurologist proactively refers Candace to a specialist at an academic center 3 hours away who has treated over 40 anti-NMDAR cases and leads an active trial site. This is Candace's first access to true disease expertise.
events.tooltip: Local neurologist refers Candace to a specialist who has treated 40+ anti-NMDAR cases and leads an active trial site — her first access to true disease expertise.

events.label: 3-Hour Travel Burden
events.description: The academic center requires in-person screening visits and monthly follow-ups. Travel time, overnight accommodations, and lost academic days create a significant practical barrier that Candace and her mother begin calculating.
events.tooltip: Academic center requires in-person screening and monthly follow-ups. Three-hour travel plus overnight stays create a significant practical burden.

sentiment_reasons: Neurologist's candid acknowledgment of her own limits is unexpectedly reassuring
Referral to an academic specialist represents the first access to true disease expertise
Travel burden is real but Candace decides it does not override the opportunity

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Honest redirection to a more qualified specialist produces a net positive emotional response despite the added burden.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The local neurologist's transparency about her limitations paradoxically increases Candace's overall trust in the care team.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Access to a specialist with 40+ anti-NMDAR cases treated gives Candace confidence she is in expert hands.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Three-hour travel to the academic center plus monthly in-person requirements creates a significant ongoing logistical load.

journey.step: Informed Consent Process and Decision Point
journey.narrative_description: At the academic center, Candace reviews an informed consent document for a maintenance immunotherapy trial. The protocol is dense and cognitively demanding. She asks her mother to attend and takes the document home to review. Her key concerns are adverse event risk, the possibility of randomization to placebo, and whether participation requires pausing her academic reintegration.

inflection_detail.label: Consent Comprehension and Enrollment Decision
inflection_detail.description: For patients with residual cognitive effects of anti-NMDAR encephalitis, the standard informed consent process may present a disproportionate barrier. Candace's high health literacy partially compensates, but cognitive fatigue and anxiety around the decision remain significant. This is both a patient support opportunity and a site-level protocol design issue.
inflection_detail.sponsor_opportunity: Develop plain-language, layered consent materials adapted for patients with cognitive sequelae. Include explicit timelines for academic or occupational impact. Consider a 'trial companion' resource — a peer mentor who has participated in a similar trial — to supplement the traditional informed consent conversation.
inflection_detail.data_needed: Drop-off rate between initial trial inquiry and enrollment in autoimmune encephalitis populations; proportion of patients who cite protocol complexity or cognitive burden as barrier to enrollment.

paths.label: Supported Enrollment with Clear Expectations
paths.outcome: With accessible consent materials and a peer navigator, Candace enrolls. Her participation is anchored in a clear schedule that accommodates her academic timeline. She becomes a strong protocol adherent and an informal advocate for the trial within patient communities.
paths.key_driver: Simplified, visually structured consent materials; accommodating site coordinator; predictable visit schedule that integrates with her academic calendar.

paths.label: Cognitive and Logistical Barrier to Enrollment
paths.outcome: The complexity of the consent process and uncertainty about placebo randomization overwhelm Candace's decision-making capacity at this stage of recovery. She defers, intending to revisit — but without follow-up from the site, the window closes as she gradually stabilizes and perceives less urgency.
paths.key_driver: Standard consent format not adapted for cognitive sequelae; lack of a patient navigator; academic reintegration concerns unaddressed by site team.

journey.quote: I want to do this. I just need to actually understand what I'm agreeing to — and I need to know it won't set back everything I've worked to rebuild.

events.label: Dense Consent Document Exceeds Cognitive Capacity
events.description: The 28-page informed consent document is cognitively overwhelming for Candace at her current recovery stage. She cannot fully process adverse event descriptions and placebo randomization language in a single session.
events.tooltip: 28-page consent document overwhelms Candace's current cognitive capacity. Adverse event descriptions and placebo language cannot be fully processed in one session.

events.label: Trial Peer Navigator Introduced
events.description: The academic site coordinator connects Candace with a patient who completed the same trial protocol. This peer navigator helps decode consent language, shares personal experience with the visit schedule, and reduces Candace's anxiety about the unknown.
events.tooltip: Site coordinator connects Candace with a trial peer navigator who decodes consent language and shares first-hand experience of the visit schedule.

events.label: Enrollment Decision Made
events.description: With her mother present and consent questions answered across two sessions, Candace signs the informed consent form and is formally enrolled. She describes the moment as the first decision in over a year that felt fully hers.
events.tooltip: Across two consent sessions with her mother present, Candace signs and formally enrolls — describing it as the first decision in over a year that felt fully hers.

sentiment_reasons: Dense consent document exceeds Candace's current cognitive processing capacity
Peer navigator introduction dramatically reduces anxiety about the unknown
Ultimately signing and enrolling feels like the first autonomous decision in over a year

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The enrollment decision is emotionally complex — anxiety about placebo randomization balanced against hope and agency.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Site coordinator's introduction of a peer navigator demonstrates patient-centered care, increasing trust in the research team.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Peer navigator and two-session consent process allow Candace to engage with the protocol on her own terms.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Enrollment requires absorbing a demanding visit schedule that must be reconciled with her academic reintegration timeline.

profile.name: Maggie W.
profile.bio_1: Maggie recently retired when her symptoms began interfering with her ability to work safely. her diagnosis came as a shock and he relies heavily on her healthcare providers to guide treatment decisions. While he values clear explanations and reassurance, he often feels uncertain navigating new medical systems and terminology.
profile.goal1: Slow disease progression and maintain physical independence.
profile.goal2: Understand treatment expectations and potential side effects.
profile.goal3: Reduce the burden on her family as he navigates the disease.
profile.barrier1: Limited familiarity with medical terminology and treatment pathways.
profile.barrier2: Transportation challenges for frequent appointments and evaluations.
profile.barrier3: Anxiety about how quickly the disease may progress.

journey.narrative_description: Karl first notices subtle balance changes and fatigue. He is concerned but assumes it may be age-related. She trusts her providers and believes the issue will be manageable.
journey.quote: I noticed something small, but I didn’t think it would turn into this.
sentiment_reasons: Early symptoms are noticed but not yet alarming
Maggie enters care with high provider trust and manageable logistics
No clear framework yet for what the symptoms represent

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Initial concern is moderate — Maggie is attentive but not yet distressed by her symptoms.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Maggie's care-dependent orientation means she enters the system with strong, deferential trust in providers.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Limited medical familiarity means Maggie can describe symptoms but struggles to contextualize them.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Recent retirement removes work-related scheduling conflict, but transportation limitations are an early constraint.

journey.narrative_description: When symptoms fluctuate or temporarily improve, Karl feels reassured. Hope increases and she interprets the instability as something benign or reversible.
journey.quote: When it improves, I feel like maybe everything is still under control.
sentiment_reasons: Temporary improvement is interpreted as reassurance
Maggie is predisposed to defer to optimistic interpretations
Family concern is not yet elevated

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Symptom relief provides genuine emotional respite and reduces urgency around seeking further evaluation.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Improvement is credited to the current care team, reinforcing trust in their approach.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Periods of improvement reduce Maggie's motivation to learn more about the condition.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Fewer symptoms reduce appointment frequency, temporarily easing logistics.

journey.narrative_description: During early appointments, Karl struggles to clearly describe her symptoms. She leaves feeling partially heard but uncertain. Trust begins to soften as explanations feel incomplete.
journey.quote: I leave appointments with more questions than answers.
sentiment_reasons: Early appointments leave important questions unanswered
Maggie struggles with medical terminology and incomplete explanations
She leaves uncertain but does not yet feel dismissed

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Mild frustration from incomplete communication begins to surface beneath Maggie's generally compliant exterior.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust remains intact but begins to soften when explanations don't match her felt experience.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Terminology barriers reduce Maggie's ability to ask the right questions or understand what she's being told.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Appointments are manageable, but their frequency is starting to require transportation planning.

journey.narrative_description: As symptoms continue progressing, Karl feels discouraged and confused. The lack of clarity lowers her confidence in her own understanding and in the care pathway.
journey.quote: I feel like I’m slipping, and no one can tell me why.
sentiment_reasons: Progressive worsening without clear explanation generates confusion and despair
Maggie's reliance on providers means lack of answers feels like abandonment
Family begins to notice and express concern, adding emotional pressure

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Unexplained progression triggers a significant emotional drop, particularly given Maggie's reliance on provider guidance.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Inconclusive responses to worsening symptoms erode Maggie's foundational trust in the current care pathway.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Unable to self-research or self-advocate effectively, Maggie feels increasingly passive and powerless.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Worsening symptoms begin affecting daily function and increasing the logistical demands of care visits.

journey.narrative_description: The referral to a neurologist restores cautious optimism. Karl believes expert input may finally provide answers, even though the logistics feel burdensome.
journey.quote: Maybe this specialist will finally connect the dots.
sentiment_reasons: Neurologist referral represents hope for expert answers
Maggie trusts the specialist system implicitly
Logistics of accessing specialist care are a real concern

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Anticipation of specialist expertise restores cautious optimism after a frustrating primary care experience.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The referral itself signals to Maggie that the system is responding to her needs.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Maggie feels no more empowered but is hopeful the specialist will provide clarity she hasn't been able to find herself.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Transportation to a specialist adds meaningful logistical complexity for someone with limited mobility resources.

journey.narrative_description: Receiving an initial but incorrect diagnosis is destabilizing. Karl feels surprised and unsettled. When treatment does not help, doubt grows—both in the system and in her own interpretation of symptoms.
journey.quote: This doesn’t feel right—why isn’t anything improving?
sentiment_reasons: Receiving an incorrect diagnosis is disorienting and frightening
When treatment fails to help, Maggie loses confidence in the diagnostic process
Provider-dependent orientation means misdiagnosis is a particularly significant trust rupture

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: A wrong diagnosis and treatment non-response create significant emotional destabilization for someone who relies heavily on providers.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Misdiagnosis represents a concrete breach — Maggie's trust in specialist accuracy drops markedly.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Unable to independently evaluate the diagnosis, Maggie has no pathway to challenge or correct it herself.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Time and resources spent treating the wrong condition represent a significant logistical and financial setback.

journey.narrative_description: Seeking a second opinion feels proactive. Karl regains some agency and begins researching more deeply, determined to reach diagnostic clarity.
journey.quote: I need another opinion—I can’t just accept uncertainty.
sentiment_reasons: Pursuing a second opinion feels bold for someone who typically defers to providers
Maggie experiences this as taking active control of a frightening situation
The act itself partially restores emotional equilibrium

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Taking proactive action against a perceived system failure provides meaningful emotional agency and relief.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Trust is being rebuilt — selectively, directed at whoever can provide the clarity she needs.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Seeking a second opinion, even if facilitated by family, represents a growth in self-advocacy capacity.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Second opinion adds a new specialist relationship and scheduling layer to an already complex care picture.

journey.narrative_description: The Parkinson’s diagnosis brings both relief and grief. Relief that there is finally a label; grief over what it implies. She feels overwhelmed by the long arc of disease progression.

inflection_detail.label: Diagnosis Decision Point
inflection_detail.description: Receiving a confirmed Parkinson's diagnosis is the central inflection of Karl's journey. How she and her care team respond in the immediate aftermath — emotionally, informationally, and logistically — determines whether she moves toward empowered self-management or retreats into isolation and fear.
inflection_detail.sponsor_opportunity: Develop a diagnosis-day support package for HCPs: standardized emotional framing language, a patient-facing 'What happens next' one-pager, and a warm handoff to a patient navigator or advocacy organization.
inflection_detail.data_needed: Rate of clinical trial awareness at time of confirmed PD diagnosis; proportion of patients who receive disease management education within 30 days of diagnosis.

paths.label: Supported Transition to Self-Management
paths.outcome: Karl receives structured post-diagnosis education, is connected to an advocacy community, and begins shared decision-making with her neurologist. Trial awareness is introduced within 60 days.
paths.key_driver: Proactive HCP communication, patient navigator support, and strong family involvement.

paths.label: Emotional Withdrawal and Disengagement
paths.outcome: Without structured support, grief dominates. Karl delays follow-up appointments, withdraws from her caregiver network, and is unlikely to engage with trial options for 12+ months.
paths.key_driver: Information overload at point of diagnosis, lack of emotional scaffolding, and unaddressed logistical concerns.

journey.quote: It’s a relief to know, but it’s also overwhelming to accept.
sentiment_reasons: A confirmed diagnosis brings relief from uncertainty but triggers anticipatory grief
The progressive nature of Parkinson's is emotionally overwhelming to confront
Maggie's low health literacy makes managing the informational demands of the diagnosis particularly hard

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Relief at finally having a name is quickly overtaken by grief about what a Parkinson's diagnosis means long-term.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Maggie now relies even more heavily on her neurologist as the primary guide through an overwhelming information landscape.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: The complexity of Parkinson's management far exceeds Maggie's current self-management confidence.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Anticipating ongoing specialist visits, medications, and potential physical therapy creates overwhelming near-term planning demands.

journey.narrative_description: Online communities provide emotional validation and practical insights. Karl feels less alone and gains confidence in navigating future decisions.
journey.quote: Talking to others makes me feel like I can actually manage this.
sentiment_reasons: Online communities provide emotional normalization and reduce isolation
Practical tips from peers help Maggie navigate care decisions she finds confusing
Hearing success stories increases confidence in the path ahead

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Peer connection and shared experience produce a meaningful positive shift after the emotional weight of diagnosis.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Community reinforcement of positive provider experiences bolsters Maggie's trust in specialist-led care.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Peer knowledge fills in gaps that formal clinical communication has left, gradually building Maggie's disease literacy.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Community members share practical strategies for managing transportation, scheduling, and family coordination.

journey.narrative_description: When evaluating clinical trials, Karl feels cautiously hopeful. She trusts her neurologist but worries about the physical and logistical demands participation may require.
journey.quote: I want to explore every option, but I’m worried about what it will take.
sentiment_reasons: A clinical trial represents hope for better disease control
Maggie trusts her neurologist's recommendation above all other sources
Practical demands of trial participation are a significant concern

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Cautious optimism about trial participation is tempered by worry about what the logistical and physical demands will require.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Maggie delegates the trial evaluation almost entirely to her neurologist, reflecting high but passive trust.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Maggie can understand the general concept of a trial but relies on her provider to interpret specifics and eligibility.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Transportation constraints and appointment frequency make trial participation a genuinely difficult logistical calculation.

profile.name: Kristen T.
profile.quote: You’re constantly balancing protection and independence. Every fall could mean a fracture, but you still want them to have a normal life.
profile.bio_1: Kristen is the primary caregiver for her young child diagnosed with osteogenesis imperfecta (OI), a rare genetic disorder characterized by brittle bones and frequent fractures. From infancy, she has navigated repeated hospital visits, surgeries, and ongoing physical therapy. She has become highly knowledgeable about OI management, but the emotional and logistical burden remains constant. She operates in a state of continuous vigilance, balancing safety with her child’s quality of life.
profile.goal1: Minimize fractures and complications while enabling a normal childhood experience.
profile.goal2: Coordinate care across orthopedic surgeons, geneticists, and rehabilitation teams.
profile.goal3: Build long-term independence and resilience for her child.
profile.barrier1: Constant risk of injury requiring immediate medical intervention.
profile.barrier2: High logistical burden from frequent appointments, surgeries, and therapy.
profile.barrier3: Emotional strain of maintaining vigilance without limiting the child’s development.

journey.narrative_description: Early fractures lead to rapid escalation into specialist care. Kristen feels shock and fear, trying to understand a rare diagnosis while managing immediate medical needs.
journey.quote: We didn’t even know what OI was — suddenly we were in a world of specialists and uncertainty.
sentiment_reasons: Sudden entry into a world of rare disease specialists is shocking and disorienting
Kristen has no prior framework for understanding OI or its implications
Fear for her child's safety and future dominates the emotional landscape

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The combination of fear, shock, and uncertainty at initial diagnosis produces the lowest emotional point of the journey.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Despite the shock, early specialist engagement is high-quality, providing a meaningful anchor of trust.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Kristen knows nothing about OI — she cannot yet participate meaningfully in care decisions.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Immediate medical management consumes all logistical resources; normal family life is suspended.

journey.narrative_description: Genetic confirmation provides clarity but reinforces the lifelong nature of the condition. Kristen begins shifting from reaction to long-term planning.
sentiment_reasons: Genetic confirmation replaces frightening ambiguity with a defined condition
Understanding the lifelong nature of OI triggers both relief and grief
Kristen begins shifting from crisis response to long-term planning

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Diagnostic clarity reduces acute panic but introduces the weight of lifelong caregiving as a new emotional reality.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Specialist confidence in the genetic diagnosis increases Kristen's trust in the medical team's competence.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: The genetic framework gives Kristen her first concrete footing for understanding the condition.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Long-term care planning begins to take shape, though the full scope of ongoing demands is not yet visible.

journey.narrative_description: Frequent fractures create a repetitive cycle of ER visits, casting, and recovery. Kristen experiences exhaustion and hypervigilance, constantly anticipating the next injury.
journey.quote: You’re always waiting for the next break. It never fully leaves your mind.
sentiment_reasons: Repeated ER visits create a relentless cycle of crisis and recovery
Hypervigilance becomes a constant emotional state that never fully turns off
The physical and emotional toll on Kristen as a caregiver is severe

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The unrelenting cycle of fractures and emergencies produces severe caregiver emotional exhaustion.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: ER teams provide acute care competently, but the reactive nature of care reduces confidence in prevention.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Kristen is beginning to learn the warning signs of fractures but cannot prevent them — which erodes her sense of efficacy.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Frequent ER visits, missed work, and constant care coordination push logistical capacity to its lowest point.

journey.narrative_description: Surgical interventions offer structural stability but introduce new risks. Trust in specialized providers increases despite ongoing fear.
sentiment_reasons: Surgical intervention represents a proactive step toward structural stability
Trust in the orthopedic surgical team is high despite ongoing fear
The procedure introduces new risks but also new hope for reduced fracture frequency

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Fear about surgical risk is present but partially offset by hope for improved structural outcomes.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Engagement with a specialized orthopedic surgeon significantly increases Kristen's confidence in the care team.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Surgery gives Kristen a clearer role as care coordinator — she can prepare, advocate, and follow a structured plan.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Surgical recovery introduces an intensive short-term logistics period — hospital stays, post-op care, and rehabilitation coordination.

journey.narrative_description: Rehabilitation introduces progress and optimism. Kristen sees incremental gains in strength and mobility, reinforcing her role as an active care coordinator.
sentiment_reasons: Incremental mobility gains in physical therapy are concretely observable and motivating
Kristen sees her child gaining strength and independence
Her role as an active care coordinator feels purposeful and effective

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Visible functional progress during rehabilitation produces a meaningful and sustained positive emotional shift.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: The rehabilitation team's structured approach and measurable outcomes build strong confidence in the PT relationship.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Kristen becomes a skilled participant in therapy planning, actively reinforcing exercises at home.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Regular therapy scheduling is demanding but more predictable than the crisis-driven ER cycle.

journey.narrative_description: Kristen navigates constant tradeoffs between protecting her child and enabling normal development. This becomes a persistent emotional tension.
journey.quote: You don’t want to hold them back, but you’re always thinking about what could happen.
sentiment_reasons: Every decision about activity or independence involves a real risk calculation
Kristen holds the tension between protection and a normal childhood as a constant emotional burden
Neither full protection nor full freedom feels right — uncertainty about the right choice persists

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: The ongoing tradeoff between safety and normalcy creates a persistent, low-level emotional tension with no clean resolution.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Kristen trusts her care team but recognizes that daily parenting decisions fall outside what any clinician can guide.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Years of caregiving experience have built strong practical knowledge, but no formula eliminates the judgment calls.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Daily adaptation to OI constraints is manageable but requires constant background cognitive load.

journey.narrative_description: Over time, Kristen becomes highly knowledgeable about OI care. She advocates effectively, coordinates care, and anticipates complications.
journey.quote: You learn everything — medications, signs of fractures, who to call. You have to.
sentiment_reasons: Kristen has become a highly informed OI advocate through accumulated experience
She can anticipate complications and navigate specialist systems with confidence
Mastery of the caregiving role provides a meaningful source of identity and purpose

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Expert caregiver identity generates sustained positive emotional engagement despite the ongoing demands of the role.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Kristen now participates as a knowledgeable partner in care decisions, elevating mutual trust with providers.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Years of accumulated OI expertise place Kristen at the highest point of self-efficacy in her journey.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Predictable care routines and established provider relationships make logistics more manageable than at any prior stage.

journey.narrative_description: Connecting with other OI families reduces isolation and provides practical strategies. Emotional burden becomes more manageable through shared experience.
sentiment_reasons: OI family communities provide practical knowledge and emotional solidarity
Shared experience reduces the isolation of managing a rare disease
Kristen transitions from receiving support to contributing it — a meaningful shift

metric_explainers.emotional_valence: Community connection produces sustained positive emotional benefit and a sense of shared purpose.
metric_explainers.provider_trust: Community members reinforce trust in established OI care standards while flagging gaps to watch for.
metric_explainers.medical_self_efficacy: Peer exchange supplements clinical knowledge and helps Kristen navigate novel caregiving challenges.
metric_explainers.logistical_capacity: Community networks provide practical resources — shared strategies for scheduling, travel, and insurance navigation.
